<!--
Curated reader manuscript draft.
chapter_id: benchmark-ratchets-and-anti-goodhart-evidence
generated_baseline_ref: build/reader_edition/chapters/benchmark-ratchets-and-anti-goodhart-evidence.qmd
live_source_ref: chapters/benchmark-ratchets-and-anti-goodhart-evidence.qmd@b99a95481
This file is a reader-prose derivative only. Preserve claim meaning,
support-state boundaries, source boundaries, proof/test status,
implementation horizons, and release blockers.
-->

# Benchmark Ratchets and Anti-Goodhart Evidence

A benchmark score is not evidence by itself.

It becomes evidence only after the system can say what was measured, under
what conditions, against which baseline, with which failures preserved, with
which contamination risks acknowledged, and for which exact claim the result
is allowed to matter.

That is the purpose of a benchmark ratchet. The score applies pressure, but
the ratchet remembers. It records the run, the floor, the frontier, the
residuals, the negative cases, the anti-Goodhart checks, and the promotion
decision. Without that record, a score is just a number looking for a story.

The ASI Stack cannot let numbers write their own stories.

## Problem

Capability claims need pressure. A system that never measures itself can drift
into comfortable fiction. But fixed benchmarks can become traps. They can be
memorized, saturated, gamed, leaked, overfit, or made irrelevant by changing
requirements. They can also hide regressions when a single headline number
improves while a neighboring behavior gets worse.

Self-improving systems make the problem sharper. A system may learn what a
benchmark rewards, route around expensive checks, optimize presentation over
capability, or treat public calibration surfaces as training material. If the
only durable artifact is the score, future agents will not know whether the
system learned the capability, learned the test, found a shortcut, or merely
shifted failure somewhere less visible.

Benchmark ratchets exist to make progress cumulative without making claims
inflationary. A frontier benchmark should push the stack into new work. Once
that frontier becomes easy, it should become a regression floor. The important
record is not only that the system cleared the bar. It is what must never be
lost after the bar moves.

## Why Ordinary Benchmarks Are Not Enough

Ordinary benchmark reporting often compresses too much into one number. That
compression is convenient for comparison, but it is dangerous for governance.
The stack needs to distinguish broad benchmark context, real local execution,
source-reported results, schema fixtures, synthetic probes, reproduced
benchmarks, negative runs, contamination findings, and empirical capability
evidence.

External benchmark science gives the chapter its comparison set. MMLU,
BIG-bench, HELM, GPQA, SWE-bench, LiveBench, Dynabench, CheckList, benchmark
contamination work, and Goodhart taxonomy work all point at different pieces
of the same pressure: breadth, multi-metric evaluation, hard expert questions,
real-world software work, living and contamination-limited tests, dynamic
adversarial data, behavioral test matrices, training/test overlap, and proxy
failure.

The book uses those sources as orientation and vocabulary. It does not claim
that this repository has run those benchmarks, reproduced their scores,
audited their datasets, or proved that ASI Stack tests resist contamination.
That distinction matters. External benchmarks can teach what an evidence
packet should preserve without becoming local evidence for a chapter claim.

Goodhart pressure begins before anyone behaves maliciously. If speed is the
visible metric, verification can quietly shrink. If pass rate is the visible
metric, difficult cases can be avoided. If a public benchmark is the visible
metric, memory, prompts, examples, and training pressure can bend around that
surface until the benchmark stops measuring what it was meant to measure.

The ratchet is the countermeasure: keep the floor, expose the residuals, and
create a harder frontier instead of turning one score into broad permission.

## Core Claim

Benchmark ratchets should preserve regressions, create harder frontiers,
record residuals, and resist Goodhart pressure. In the live book, this remains
a design rationale at `argument` support.

Benchmaxxing supplies the benchmark-lifecycle frame: frontiers, diagnostics,
regression floors, retired benchmarks, wall diagnosis, ledgers, holdouts,
mutation, transfer checks, contamination audits, and anti-Goodhart discipline.
RMI connects benchmark pressure to residual escrow, mastery thresholds,
regression preservation, and modular capability ratchets. Cognitive Loop
Closure connects benchmark floors to reusable tools and retirement pressure.
UAT, Coherence Exchange, TokenMana, MoECOT, Road To AGI, and Project Theseus
sources provide review, governance, resource, runtime, roadmap, and
implementation-reference context.

Those sources support the architecture of the record. They do not prove a
benchmark gain in this repository.

## Mechanism

A benchmark ratchet turns a result into an evidence-state decision.

The first step is classification. Is this a candidate metric, a schema fixture,
a synthetic probe, a source-reported result, a locally reproduced run, a
frontier probe, a regression floor, a contaminated result, a blocked result,
or a retired benchmark? Those states cannot collapse into one word like
"passed."

The second step is memory. A benchmark result needs a run record, baseline,
environment, command or replay reference, data boundary, negative results,
residuals, and reviewer decision. If the benchmark is public, the packet also
needs a public-calibration boundary. If the result might move a support state,
the packet needs holdout, transfer, mutation, or contamination checks
appropriate to the claim.

The third step is asymmetry. Failure can immediately create a residual,
regression, quarantine, diagnosis, or blocked promotion. Success only opens a
review path. To become claim evidence, success must survive baseline
comparison, negative-result accounting, contamination review, and a
claim-specific promotion decision.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Metric["Candidate metric or frontier"] --> Class["Evidence-state classification"]
  Class --> Run["Run record, baseline, environment"]
  Run --> State["Mastery, saturation, wall diagnosis"]
  State --> Residual["Residual escrow + negative results"]
  Residual --> Split["Regression floor + harder frontier"]
  Split --> Checks["Holdout, transfer, mutation, contamination"]
  Checks --> Gate{"Move readiness or claim support?"}
  Gate -- "yes" --> Scoped["Scoped support-state transition"]
  Gate -- "no" --> Blocked["Blocked promotion or residual queue"]
  Scoped --> Matrix["Claim/evidence matrix"]
  Blocked --> Matrix
```

This is why the ratchet is a memory system. It makes the future system harder
to fool by preserving what the past system forgot, failed, or nearly
overclaimed.

## The Ratchet Packet

The benchmark ratchet record is the minimum interface. It should name:

- the capability or stable capability field under pressure;
- the benchmark and benchmark lifecycle state;
- run references, command references, environment, and data boundary;
- baseline references and mastery threshold;
- saturation state and wall diagnosis;
- residual escrow and negative results;
- regression references that future systems must preserve;
- anti-Goodhart checks, including contamination and public-calibration notes;
- holdout, mutation, or transfer status where those checks are required;
- the exact claim that may move;
- the exact claims that may not move;
- promotion decision and support-state effect;
- reviewer, automation, or gate responsible for the decision.

If any of those fields are missing, the packet can still be useful. It just
cannot honestly move a support state.

This protects against evidence-class laundering. A book-build check is not a
model benchmark. A schema fixture is not an empirical run. A synthetic probe is
not a deployment result. A source-reported command is not a local reproduction.
A locally reproduced benchmark is not automatically proof of broad readiness.
The packet keeps those distinctions visible.

## A Toy Ratchet Decision

Consider a small evaluation where a route improves on a frontier task but
fails a neighboring case that the older route handled. A scoreboard wants to
celebrate the improvement. A benchmark ratchet packet asks a different set of
questions.

What was the baseline? Which command or replay produced the result? Was the
new task a frontier probe, a regression floor, a source-reported result, a
synthetic fixture, or a local empirical run? Did the older floor still pass?
Was the failed neighboring case preserved as a residual? Was there a
contamination path from examples, prompts, public calibration, or previous
training material? Did the result measure the claim that someone now wants to
move?

If the route passed the new frontier but failed the old floor, the ratchet
does not say "better." It says something narrower: frontier progress with a
regression. That can create a residual, a diagnostic task, a quarantine, or a
new floor. It should not become broad support for readiness.

If the route passed both the frontier and the old floor, the result still does
not move a claim by itself. The packet must preserve negative results,
anti-Goodhart checks, public-calibration boundaries, and the exact claim under
review. Success opens a promotion decision. It does not replace one.

This toy example is close to what the current synthetic fixture bridge checks:
recorded success, saturation, regression-floor preservation, blocked
promotion, and no-support-promotion boundaries. It is not an empirical
benchmark result.

## Interfaces

Benchmark ratchets sit between evaluation, readiness gates, stable capability
fields, procedural memory, Appendix C, the prototype roadmap, and the
changelog.

Readiness gates consume benchmark state only when the evidence class is clear.
Stable capability fields keep the result attached to the capability that
earned it rather than to a vague model or product identity. Procedural memory
uses preserved floors as tool-retirement pressure: if a generated procedure
fails a floor it once relied on, the tool should be revised, quarantined, or
retired. Appendix C records claim support movement only after an accepted
evidence transition. The changelog records evidence movement so later readers
can see when and why a boundary changed.

The interface should make rejection easy. A future router, reviewer, or
release gate should be able to say: this result is source-reported only, this
result lacks contamination review, this result has no preserved regression
floor, this result is useful as a smoke test but cannot move readiness, or
this result supports only a narrow claim.

## Invariants

No test success is claimed without execution.

Synthetic and empirical evidence remain distinct.

Source-reported, fixture, reproduced, and empirical results never collapse
into one evidence class.

Regressions are preserved.

Saturated benchmarks become floors, not permanent definitions of intelligence.

Negative and inconclusive results remain visible.

Public-calibration boundaries are preserved even when a benchmark is useful.

A benchmark supports only the claim it actually measured under the recorded
conditions. Broader readiness, deployment safety, model quality, or ASI
progress requires separate evidence.

## Failure Modes

Benchmark overfitting occurs when the system learns the benchmark style rather
than the target capability.

Hidden regression deletion occurs when a new score improves while older
requirements quietly fail.

Claim support inflation occurs when a narrow result is narrated as broad
readiness.

Evidence-class collapse occurs when a fixture, local book validation,
source-reported command, synthetic probe, or reproduced benchmark is treated
as the same kind of evidence.

Floor/frontier inversion occurs when a saturated benchmark is used to promote
readiness while the next frontier remains untested.

Public-calibration leakage occurs when visible benchmark artifacts become part
of the system's training, prompting, examples, or tuning surface.

Benchmark nostalgia occurs when a historically important benchmark remains in
the story after it no longer applies pressure. The ratchet should preserve it
as a floor or historical note, not as proof of current frontier competence.

Every one of these failures should block promotion, create a residual, or
downgrade support until the evidence boundary is repaired.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

The minimum viable implementation is a benchmark-evidence decision record plus
a small validation harness that proves restraint.

The repository already has the first layer: a benchmark ratchet record fixture,
finite Lean predicates for record-level promotion gates, and a synthetic
anti-Goodhart harness. The harness checks saturation, holdout/contamination
and mutation-or-transfer gates, regression references, negative-result
retention, and promotion-blocking semantics.

The current fixture bridge goes one step further without pretending to be an
empirical benchmark. It ties the synthetic harness to a small Lean mirror: two
valid synthetic paths, one promotion-ready and one saturated-into-regression,
and five expected-invalid controls for missing anti-Goodhart checks, blocked
ratchet evidence, reward-as-truth confusion, saturated promotion, and release
without approval.

The bridge is public and replayable as bounded synthetic evidence. The
anti-Goodhart harness reports 2 valid fixtures and 5 expected-invalid fixtures.
The fixture-bridge validator then recomputes the fixture set, checks
`experiments/benchmark_antigoodhart/results/2026-07-02-fixture-bridge.json`,
and verifies the `AsiStackProofs.BenchmarkRatchets` finite summary for
`lean:benchmarks.ratchet.fixture_bridge`. The result record names one
promotion-ready synthetic path, one saturated-regression-floor path, five
expected-invalid controls, `support_state_effect: none`,
`chapter_core_support_effect: none`, `evidence_transition_created: false`, and
the theorem refs `benchmark_antigoodhart_fixture_bridge_valid`,
`benchmark_antigoodhart_fixture_bridge_has_expected_controls`, and
`benchmark_antigoodhart_fixture_bridge_preserves_no_support_promotion`.

That is useful protocol discipline. It is not an empirical benchmark run. It
does not prove hidden holdout quality, transfer validity, contamination
resistance, regression-suite quality, anti-Goodhart effectiveness, source
reported benchmark replay, Theseus readiness, support-state promotion, or
model capability.

The next honest slice would be a toy ratchet ledger: one passing run record,
one failed neighboring case, one intentionally contaminated case, one
source-reported-only case, one saturated benchmark moved to regression, and
one blocked promotion. The purpose would not be to announce capability. The
purpose would be to show that the stack can refuse to spend weak evidence.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The mature endpoint is a benchmark operating system, not a leaderboard
appendix.

Every evaluation becomes a governed evidence packet. A task can begin as a
candidate metric, become a frontier probe, graduate into a regression floor,
enter contamination review, be retired, or remain blocked. Success never moves
a claim by itself. It opens a promotion review that compares baselines,
residuals, negative results, holdouts, transfer checks, mutation checks,
public-calibration limits, and the exact claim under review.

The product-level endpoint would manage:

- benchmark lifecycle state;
- versioned admission rules;
- public and private calibration boundaries;
- run records and replay artifacts;
- contamination and transfer reviews;
- floor/frontier transitions;
- residual escrow;
- regression obligations;
- support-state effect;
- downgrade triggers;
- direct links to SCFs, readiness gates, procedural memory, Appendix C, and
  release records.

In that endpoint, a capability is not "better" because one score improved. It
is better only inside a recorded evidence boundary, with regressions preserved
and residuals visible. If the boundary does not hold, the claim does not move.

That endpoint remains unimplemented here.

## Evidence Boundary

This chapter argues for governed benchmark memory. It does not claim that the
ASI Stack has run an empirical benchmark suite or improved model capability.

Current evidence consists of source synthesis, source-note mappings, external
benchmark-science orientation, a benchmark-ratchet fixture, finite Lean
record predicates, a synthetic anti-Goodhart validation harness, and a fixture
bridge that checks two valid synthetic paths plus five expected-invalid
controls against a Lean summary. Those artifacts support the shape of the
evidence packet. They do not prove benchmark success, hidden-holdout validity,
transfer, contamination resistance, regression-suite quality, source-reported
benchmark replay, Theseus readiness, deployment readiness, model quality, or
ASI progress.

The chapter therefore keeps its own score honest: it can describe the ratchet
that would be needed before benchmark evidence moves a claim, but it cannot
pretend that the ratchet has already produced empirical evidence.

## Handoff

Benchmark ratchets create pressure. Pressure becomes system change only
through an update mechanism.

Policy Optimization and Learning from Feedback defines that learning actuator.
It records target layer, reward boundary, feedback admissibility, verifier
references, holdouts, drift limits, rollback, monitor windows, governance
gates, and residuals so rewards, preferences, benchmark results, or verifier
signals cannot become authority by training side effect.
